t
Farn Polrrrca,l Pnrcrtcps C outtussloN
P.O. Box tt07 . -I28 J Strcet . Sacramento. CA 95811-0tt07
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-08u6
August 31, 2000
Dan Nguyen-Tan
440 Madrone Avenue
Chico, California 95926
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4.-00-168
Dear Mr. Nguyen-Tan:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTIONS
1. May you use an Internet service to collect on-line contributions to your
campaign account?
2. Is it permissible for your committee to accept a $5 credit for each person that
registers with the Internet service in order to contribute to you?
CONCLUSION
1. Yes, you may use an Internet service to collect on-line contributions to your
campaign account so long as you comply with the recordkeeping and disclosure
requirements of the Act described below.
2. Nothing in the Act prohibits your campaign committee from receiving a $5
credit as a "referral fee."
1 Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regutations appear at title 2, sections
18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-00-168
PageZ
FACTS
You are running for City Council in the City of Chico. You are developing a
campaign website and would like to accept online contributions. You would like to use a
free service called Palpal.com to accept online contributions. Paypal.com allows
registered users to charge their credit card to send money to another registered user of
Paypal.com's seryices. To register, users simply furnish contact information, provide a
credit card or bank account, and submit a working e-mail address. You have registered
your candidate campaign committee, "Friends of Dan Nguyen-Tan," ard provided your
campaign bank account.
A person who accesses your website could contribute to you by taking the
following steps. First, the contributor would fill out their contact information, and click
on a Palpal.com link on your website. The contributor would register for Paypal.com
and charge the contribution to their credit card.
You would then receive a message in your e-mail in-box that the contributor
would like to send you money. You would then go to Palpal.com and accept the request.
Once you accept, the contributor's credit card is charged and the amount is deposited into
your "Friends of Dan Nguyen-Tan" Paypal.com account. After a few days, the money in
your Paypal.com account would be automatically deposited into your campaigu bank
account.
Your campaign bank account monthly summary statement would show a deposit
from Paypal.com. Paypal.com charges avery small percentage for depositing money
directly into the campaign bank account. You would keep track of each person who
contributes online through Paypal.com, although those deposits do not appear on the
campaign bank statement.
Lastly, every time a visitor to your website registers with Paypal.com, your
"Friends of Dan Nguyen-Tan" Paypal.com account is credited $5 from Paypal.com as a
referral fee.
AI\ALYSIS
Internet Fundraising
Section 82015 of the Act defines "contribution" as follows:
"(a) 'Contribution' means a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan
by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that
full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes.
(b)(1) A payment made at the behest of a committee as defined in subdivision (a)
of Section 82013 is a contribution to the committee unless full and adequate
consideration is received from the committee for making the payment.


File No. A-00-168
Page 3
(2) Apayment made at the behest of a candidate is a contribution to the
candidate unless the criteria in either subparagraph (A) or (B) are satisfied:
(A) Full and adequate consideration is received from the candidate.
(B) It is clear from the surrounding circumstances that the payment was made
for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy for elective office.
***
The Act requires disclosure and recordkeeping with respect to contributions and it
prescribes the manner in which conkibutions of $100 or more may be made. (Section
84200 et seq; Section 84101; and Section 8a300(c).) Under the relevant provisions of the
Act, candidates and committees may raise contributions over the Internet, as long as the
Act's disclosure and recordkeeping requirements are met. (Section 84300; Lavin Advice
Letter, No. 4.-99-201; Bergeron Advice Letter, No. I-00-089.) 
,
We have previously advised that contributions may be made by credit card, by
means of a tele-network using the telephone company as the collection agent, and
through the use of a 900-number telephone line, provided that the procedures for
solicitation and reporting of contributions comply with the Act. (Buck-Walsh Advice
Letter, No. I-90-239; Zerbe Advice Letter, No. I-95-146.)
Applying these principles, you may use the Internet as the "collection agent"
where contributors use their credit cards as the means of payment, provided that the
procedure complies with the disclosure provisions of the Act, described below.
Disclosure
The Act requires committees to file periodic reports disclosing contributions
received and expenditures made for the purpose of supporting or opposing state and local
candidates and ballot measures. Section 84211requires that for each person who
contributes a cumulative amount of $100 or more, a committee must disclose the
following information:
The date of the contribution;
The name and address of the contributor;
If the contributor is an individual, the name of his or her employer or, if
self-employed, the name of his or her business.
If the contributor is a committee, the identification number assigned to
the committee by the Secretary of State.
The amount received from the contributor during the period covered by
the report; and
The cumulative amount received form the contributor since January I
ofthe current calendar year.
1.
2.
aJ.
4.
5
6.


File No. A-00-168
Page 4
In addition, Section 84211(d) requires committees to disclose the total amount of
contributions received from persons who have given a cumulative amount of less than
$100.
You indicate that you keep track of every contributor who gives online through
Paypal.com, but you do not indicate the level of details that are maintained in your
records. In order to be able to comply with the above rules, the Act also imposes
recordkeeping requirements.
Recordkeeping Requirements
The Act states:
"It shall be the duty of each candidate, treasurer and elected officer to maintain
such detailed accounts, records, bills and receipts that are necessarylo prepile campaign
statements and to comply with the provision of this chapter. The detailed accounts,
records, bills and receipts that are maintained shall be retained by the filer for a period
specified by the commission ... ."
Regulation 18401 (copy enclosed) sets forth the records that committees must
maintain for conkibutions.
a. Contributions under $25. Candidates, treasurers and elected officers must
keep a list of the dates and daily totals of contributions received under $25. The original
source documentation for these contributions consists of bank statements, check registers,
bank or passbooks, and any other records reflecting a continuous computation of
campaign account balances. (Regulation I 8a0 1 (a)(1 ).)
b. Contributions of $25 or more but less than $100. In addition to the daily
totals required for contributions under $25, for contributions of $25 or more but less than
$100, candidates, treasurers and elected officers must keep records of the date of each
contribution, the amount, and the full name and street address of the contributor. The
records must also contain the cumulative amount received from the contributor and
speciff whether the contribution is monetary or nonmonetary.
The original source documentation for these contributions includes bank
statements, check registers and passbooks, discussed above, and copies of contributor's
checks, cashier's checks, money orders, wire kansfers, deposits or duplicate deposit slips,
and any other documents reflecting all items deposited and all deposits made to any
campaign bank account. Source documentation includes contributor cards, letters of
transmittal and notices received from contributors.
c. Contributions of $100 or more. For these contributions, candidates,
treasurers and elected officers must maintain all the information required for
contributions above $25, but less than $100, and in addition, must include the occupation
and employer of any contributor (or if the contributor is self-employed, the name of the
principal place of business of the contributor).


File No. A-00-168
Page 5
The original source documentation shall consist of all items listed above for
smaller contributions, and in addition, all communications sent by the committee to
obtain the occupation and employer information.
d. Contributions through an intermediary or agent. Section 84302 requires
that anyone acting as an intermediary or agent of another in making a contribution of
$100 or more disclose his or her own name, street address, occupation and employer
information, and also disclose the same information regarding the true source of the
contribution. Information concerning both the intermediary and the contributor must be
disclosed on the committee's campaign statement.
In this situation, contributors will make credit card payments to Paypal.com. By
receiving credit card payments from contributors on behalf of your corymittee, depositing
the payments, and then transmifting those funds to your committee, Paypal.com is acting
as an intermediary under the Act. (See Wechsler Advice Letter, No. I-96-173.) On your
committee campaign report, the contributions of $100 or more should be reported from
the individual contributors, as well as Paypal.com as an intermediary.
e. Credit card transactions. For credit card transactions, the entire amount
charged to each contributor's credit card is the amount of the contribution. Your
committee should not subtract any fees retained or charged by the credit card company.2
For reporting purposes, the date the confribution is received is the date the credit card
transaction is authorized by the contributor, not the date the committee actually receives
the monetary contribution. In addition, you committee must ensure that the required
information on each contributor, including the date of the contribution, is collected in a
timely fashion either by tracking the information on the committee's website, or by
ensuring that Palpal.com fonvards the information to the committee. (Buck-Walsh
Advice Leffer, No. I-90-239.)
Referral Fee
Nothing in the Act prohibits your committee from receiving a "referral fee" from
Paypal.com, so long as this is a standard referral fee given by Palpal.com. Section
84211(k) requires that you disclose the amount and source of any miscellaneous receipt
of income to the committee. These amounts are disclosed on Schedule I of your
statement.
2 The service fee charged or retained by Paypal.com is considered an expenditure of the committee.
Therefore, any amounts charged or withheld by the Internet company must be reported by the committee as
expenditures at the time the fees are deducted or charged. (Zerbe Advice Letter, No. I-95-146.)


File No. A-00-168
Page 6
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(er6) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant
Counsel
By: Deborah L. Allison
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
LM:DLA:klw
Enclosure
